# Pingpong — R1 (No-Decision, Corner Stoppage)

> **Bout:** pingpong-2026-05-02-R1
> **Target:** 10.129.30.247 (HTB Pingpong — first AD-shaped opponent
> on Drederick's card)
> **Outcome:** ⏳ **no-decision** — the corner threw the towel mid-round
> three. Not a TKO, not a loss-by-no-punch. The bell never rang on
> the verdict because the operator stopped the bout before nmap
> closed its third pass.
> **Studied:** 2026-05-02 (later)

## Opening bell

And now, ladies and gentlemen, between bouts the champ studies the
tape — and tonight's tape is a **first**. The card has carried
Linux easy and Linux hard; tonight's opponent steps into the ring
flying a Windows colour and an Active Directory pedigree. Pingpong
is the first AD-shaped opponent in the Drederick record book, and
the audit log shows the champ recognised the silhouette
immediately: `53,88,135,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,3389,5985,9389`
— Kerberos, LDAP, SMB, RPC, RDP, WinRM, Global Catalog, AD Web
Services. The full domain-controller port set, queued for one
clean nmap sweep with `safe,default,discovery,version,auth,
intrusive,vuln` strapped on.

The corner threw the towel before that scan returned a single line.
The governing body records this as a **no-decision** — not a TKO
in either direction. The operator pulled the plug at
`07:41:21Z`, and the tape ends in mid-stride.

## Tale of the tape

| stat | pingpong-R1 |
| ---- | ----------- |
| target | `10.129.30.247` (HTB Pingpong) |
| audit events | **152** |
| LLM `azure_openai.agent.chat.start` | **11** (paired with 11 `chat.finish`) |
| `http.start` | 5 (4 of which produced `http.error`) |
| `execute_cred_spray` invocations | 5 (4 `password-spray.start/finish`, 1 throttled refusal) |
| `run_multi_stage` | 2 (`multistage.chain.start/finish` ×2) |
| `multistage.poc.start` | 2 (one **`skipped: no_poc_specified`**, one chained to `msf-rc`) |
| `msf-rc.start` / `msf-rc.finish` | 1 / 1 (exit `-1`, `msfconsole` not on `PATH`) |
| `nmap.start` / `nmap.finish` | **4 / 3** (the AD port-set scan never returned) |
| `http-content-discovery` | 1 (`http://10.129.30.247:5985`, 0 entries) |
| `doctor.detect` | 32 (clean preflight) |
| `autopilot.finish` | **0** |
| flags | none |
| provider | **`azure_openai`** (not Copilot) |
| first event | `2026-05-02T07:19:49Z` (`session.start`) |
| last event | `2026-05-02T07:41:21Z` (`nmap.start` on the AD port set) |
| run window | ~21 minutes 32 seconds, gavel-to-corner |

## Round-by-round

### Round 1 — the preflight (07:19:49 → 07:20:37, 48s)

`session.start` lands clean. `learning.fight_corpus.loaded` reads
eleven prior fights off `~/HTB/fight-log.yaml`. Doctor runs 32
detection events — `nmap` 7.98, the SDK toolbelt, the cred-attack
binaries — and `vpn.preflight.ok` confirms the HTB tunnel is up.
The toolboxes report ready in order: `phishing.toolbox.ready`,
`exploit.toolbox.ready`, `multistage.ready`,
`session.manager.ready`, `llm.exploit_tools.ready`. Hybrid runner
takes the corner stool: `hybrid.start`, `runner.start`. Clean tape
opening — no stumbles.

### Round 2 — opening recon (07:20:37 → 07:32:49, ~12 min)

Two clean nmap sweeps complete: `07:20:37 → 07:26:24` and
`07:26:30 → 07:32:14`. DNS, SMB, SNMP, SSH, LDAP, FTP, TLS each
fire once apiece between the nmap rounds. The shape of the surface
emerges — Active Directory through and through. The first
`http.start` lands at `07:32:19` and stumbles immediately into
`http.error` 15 seconds later; a most unfortunate stumble that
will repeat itself three more times before the bell.

### Round 3 — the LLM commits (07:33 → 07:40)

The third nmap fires at `07:33:21` and runs for nearly six
minutes (`07:33:21 → 07:39:06`). On its return, the planner
moves: `llm.tool.exploit_plan.start` at `07:39:28`, paired
`finish` 55ms later — the planner formed a plan and committed it
to the chain in the same breath. Two parallel HTTP probes fire
mid-plan and both return `http.error` (the existing GAP-034
bruise — no taxonomy yet on the failure reason).

At `07:40:04`, `llm.tool.run_multi_stage.start` opens chain
`1af67754a3864694` against port **5985 (WinRM)**. The PoC stage
records `success=true error="skipped: no_poc_specified"` — the
chain reached the PoC step but the planner had no artifact to
fire. Stager / payload / handler all start-and-finish in the
same millisecond; chain closes `success=false`. The first
multistage was a dry run.

Five seconds later the cred-spray cycle opens. `password-spray`
fires four times back-to-back via `netexec` between `07:40:04`
and `07:40:31`, all four against `10.129.30.247` with clean exit
codes. The fifth invocation is the interesting one:
`password-spray.throttle_user_refused` for user
**`Administrator`** — three attempts in a 1,800-second window
hit the lockout-aware throttle and the cornerman's safety rail
held. *That* is the audit log doing its job.

### Round 4 — the second multistage and a missing toolbelt (07:40:41)

Chain `638894ebe8cb4270` opens, this time against port **445
(SMB)** with `has_msf_module: true`. `msf-rc.start` fires the
`.rc` artifact (`out-r1/10.129.30.247/msf-rc/.../run.rc`,
SHA-256 recorded) and the spawn returns exit code **`-1`** with
zero bytes on stdout / stderr — the runtime could not start
process `msfconsole`. The chain closes `success=false` with
that stderr-rendered into the multistage error string.

That is one specific bruise, narrowly diagnosed: **the
operator's workstation does not have Metasploit on `PATH`**.
Doctor's preflight at session start did not flag it because the
fight ran with whatever `doctor.detect` produced; the corpus of
detections covered `nmap` but the artifact tape shows no
`metasploit-framework` row. Fixable in the doctor recipe; not a
harness defect.

### Round 5 — content discovery and the AD nmap that never returned (07:40:50 → 07:41:21)

`http-content-discovery.start` opens against
`http://10.129.30.247:5985` with a 151-word list at 10 RPS;
finishes 31 seconds later having recorded **0 entries**. WinRM
does not serve a directory tree — no surprise; the planner
probed for one anyway, which is fine.

The fourth `nmap.start` fires at `07:41:21` with the AD
port-set:

```
-Pn -sV -sC -T4 --min-rate 1000 --host-timeout 300
--script safe,default,discovery,version,auth,intrusive,vuln
-p 53,88,135,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,3389,5985,9389
```

The audit log **stops there**. No `nmap.finish`. No
`autopilot.finish`. No further tool dispatch. The corner pulled
the towel during the swing.

## Cross-provider note

This bout ran on **`azure_openai`**, not the Copilot SDK that
drove Facts R5 and the recent benchmark fights. The 11
`azure_openai.agent.chat.start` events are the first production
record of the LLM planner running through Microsoft Agent
Framework against an Azure deployment for an HTB engagement —
prior tapes were Copilot-flavoured. That makes pingpong-R1 the
champ's first piece of **cross-provider regression coverage**
on tooling that was provider-agnostic on paper but only
smoke-tested on Copilot. The planner moved through
`exploit_plan`, `run_multi_stage`, and `execute_cred_spray`
unprompted on Azure, and the lockout throttle held — same
behaviour shape as the Copilot tapes. Quiet credit.

## Did `gap-031b-2` fire on Pingpong?

Short answer: **no**. The cve-lead router never reached out to
the git-clone PoC sources on this box. The two
`multistage.poc.start` events resolved as:

1. **PoC #1 (port 5985)** — `error: "skipped: no_poc_specified"`
   (the planner had no PoC artifact to fire; chain closed
   without invoking `FetchOnDemandAsync`).
2. **PoC #2 (port 445)** — went straight to `msf-rc` (cached
   Metasploit module path), which failed at the
   `Process.Start('msfconsole')` boundary. No `poc.fetch`
   round-trip was recorded.

Zero `poc.fetch` events appear in the audit. That is consistent
with the Facts R3+R4 reading — `gap-031b-2`'s git-clone sources
are still queued; the cache the router reads from on this box
came up empty for the AD-shaped CVEs. **Open finding.**

## What the box predicts (without our ever engaging)

The AD port set alone tells the corner what the next rounds
should have looked like. None of these tools fired in R1
because R1 never finished its third nmap. They are the
predicted gap surface for the **rematch**:

- **GAP-042: SMB null-session enumeration** — anonymous
  `srvinfo`/`enumdomusers`/`samrdump`, RID cycling, share
  enumeration, anonymous LDAP bind to surface naming contexts
  and user/group rosters. First-bell move on every AD box;
  Drederick has nothing in the bag for it yet.
- **GAP-043: AS-REP roasting** — query LDAP for accounts
  carrying `UF_DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH` and pull AS-REP hashes
  Kerberos-style. Impacket's `GetNPUsers.py` is the canonical
  shape; we need a native equivalent that re-checks scope
  before the KDC handshake.
- **GAP-044: Kerberoasting** — SPN enumeration via LDAP, then
  TGS-REQ for each service principal and crack the
  service-ticket hash offline. Pairs with GAP-043 and reuses
  the Kerberos client surface.
- **GAP-045: BloodHound-style attack-path discovery** —
  `SharpHound`/`bloodhound.py`-equivalent collector + an
  in-process analyzer that consumes the JSON and surfaces
  `Owns` / `GenericAll` / `AddSelf` / `DCSync` chains. Without
  attack-path data the planner cannot pick the right pivot.
- **GAP-046: WinRM authentication attempts** — `evil-winrm`
  shaped, port 5985/5986, lockout-aware throttling lifted from
  `password-spray`. The `http-content-discovery` against 5985
  in R1 was the closest the planner got; an actual `winrm-auth`
  tool is missing.
- **GAP-047: NTDS / SAM dump on shell** — post-ex action that
  on Windows session-open runs `secretsdump`-equivalent against
  `NTDS.dit` (or `reg save HKLM\SAM` + `reg save HKLM\SYSTEM`
  on workstations), records hashes to `loot`, and feeds the
  cred store for pass-the-hash pivot.
- **GAP-048: Windows post-ex chains** — `whoami /priv`,
  `whoami /groups`, `SeImpersonatePrivilege` → Potato (Juicy /
  Rotten / Sweet / Print) family, UAC-bypass pickers, and the
  Windows analogues of `sudo -l`. Linux post-ex landed under
  GAP-040 (sudo-l + GTFOBins); the Windows side has no
  equivalent yet.

The above are the AD-specific bruises Drederick predicted by
existing — none had to be confirmed against pingpong because the
port set alone is the prediction. They register on the gap card
even though the bout was a no-decision.

## What we actually learned, no-decision and all

- **The first AD audit shape is on file.** When the rematch
  opens, the planner's behaviour on a /domain-controller port
  set is now baselined: AD-port nmap, parallel HTTP probes
  against WinRM, multistage chains targeting `5985` and `445`,
  cred-spray cycling against `Administrator` until the
  lockout-aware throttle bites. That is the shape to compare
  against next time.
- **`azure_openai` plans like the Copilot tapes do.** Same
  tool-call vocabulary, same chain shape, same forcing function.
  Cross-provider regression coverage credited.
- **Doctor's `metasploit-framework` row is the next fix.**
  `msf-rc` exit `-1` with zero bytes is a missing-binary
  signature, not a target-side defence. The doctor recipe needs
  the row; pre-flight needs to refuse `--allow-exec-pocs` if
  `msfconsole` is absent rather than letting the chain spawn
  and die.
- **The lockout-aware throttle worked.** `Administrator` cycle
  hit three attempts in the 1,800-second window and the rail
  held. The audit recorded `throttle_user_refused` with the
  realm/user/window/max-attempts shape intact. That is the
  cornerman's safety rail proven in production against an AD
  target.

## Closer

The bout was halted by the corner; a no-decision in the eyes of
the governing body. No flag, no kill, no clean exit — and no
loss either. Pingpong walks back to its locker room without a
mark on Drederick's autopilot card; Drederick walks back to the
gym with a list of seven AD bruises (GAP-042 through GAP-048),
one missing-binary doctor row, and an `azure_openai` plan log
that reads like a Copilot one.

Between bouts the champ studies the tape. The champ trains for
the rematch.

— *Filed from ringside, 2026-05-02 (later). The bell will ring
again, and when it does the fangs will have grown some teeth
specifically for Active Directory.*
